condition_overview.summary: Treatment-Resistant Depression (TRD) is defined as Major Depressive Disorder that has failed to respond to at least two adequate antidepressant trials of different pharmacological classes, each at therapeutic dose for a minimum of four weeks. TRD represents approximately 30% of MDD cases and is associated with chronic course, profound functional impairment, elevated suicide risk, and substantial socioeconomic burden. Specialised neuromodulation therapies — including accelerated rTMS/iTBS, deep TMS, and FDA PMA-approved implanted VNS — represent the highest-evidence interventions for this population.
condition_overview.prevalence: Approximately 30% of patients with MDD do not respond to two adequate antidepressant trials and qualify for a TRD designation. Population prevalence of TRD is estimated at 2–3% globally. TRD accounts for a disproportionate share of depression-related disability, healthcare utilisation, and suicide mortality.

phenotype_clusters.name: Severe Anhedonic TRD
phenotype_clusters.description: Profound and persistent inability to experience pleasure, motivation, or reward responsiveness despite multiple treatment attempts. Associated with mesolimbic dopaminergic hypofunction and poor response to serotonergic monotherapy. May respond better to dopaminergic augmentation strategies alongside neuromodulation.
phenotype_clusters.qeeg_signature: Reduced frontal beta, attenuated reward-related ERP components, persistent frontal alpha asymmetry

phenotype_clusters.name: Chronic Course TRD
phenotype_clusters.description: Depressive episode duration exceeding 2 years (chronic MDD) with inadequate response to multiple treatment modalities. Associated with structural brain changes, hippocampal volume reduction, and cognitive impairment. Higher Maudsley Staging scores (Stage III-IV).
phenotype_clusters.qeeg_signature: Widespread delta/theta excess, reduced alpha reactivity, diffuse cortical slowing

phenotype_clusters.name: Mixed Anxiety-Depression TRD
phenotype_clusters.description: Persistent depressive disorder with clinically significant comorbid anxiety that has failed to respond to multiple agents. Anxiety component may limit tolerability of excitatory TMS protocols at high intensities. Consider anxiolytic support or bilateral protocol.
phenotype_clusters.qeeg_signature: Elevated right frontal beta, frontal theta excess, reduced posterior alpha, right frontal alpha asymmetry

phenotype_clusters.name: Melancholic TRD
phenotype_clusters.description: Melancholic subtype of MDD (pervasive anhedonia, psychomotor retardation, diurnal variation, early morning awakening) that has failed antidepressant treatment. Often associated with higher HAM-D somatic subscale scores. May benefit from deep TMS targeting deeper DLPFC layers.
phenotype_clusters.qeeg_signature: Widespread delta slowing, disrupted sleep architecture EEG patterns, severe frontal asymmetry

severity_levels: Stage II (2 failed trials — TRD threshold)
Stage IV (4+ failed trials — VNS threshold)
symptom_clusters: Persistent depressed mood despite ≥2 adequate antidepressant trials
Anhedonia and reward processing impairment (often profound)
Chronic fatigue and psychomotor retardation or agitation
Elevated suicide risk (chronic course increases cumulative risk)
Sleep disturbance (typically insomnia with early morning awakening)
condition_overview.neurobiology: TRD involves not only the left DLPFC hypoactivity and sgACC hyperactivity characteristic of MDD, but also dysregulation of deeper limbic circuits, reduced hippocampal neurogenesis, inflammatory dysregulation, and altered HPA axis function. The sgACC (Brodmann Area 25) is the key hub in chronic depression and is the primary target of surgical DBS for TRD. VNS (vagus nerve stimulation) modulates the locus coeruleus–norepinephrine system and indirectly normalises amygdalar and limbic activity over weeks to months. Accelerated TMS protocols (SAINT/Stanford protocol) deliver higher total pulse doses over condensed timeframes, producing rapid neuroplastic changes via BDNF-dependent mechanisms. MRI-guided TMS targeting of the DLPFC anti-correlated with sgACC maximises DLPFC–sgACC circuit engagement and improves remission rates.
comorbidities: Anxiety disorders (GAD, panic, social anxiety — very high comorbidity in TRD)
Personality disorders (particularly cluster C and borderline PD)
PTSD (treatment interference — may require sequential trauma treatment first)
Substance use disorders (alcohol most common — lowers treatment response)
Inflammatory or autoimmune conditions (immune dysregulation linked to TRD)
differential_diagnoses: Inadequately treated MDD (insufficient dose or duration — pseudo-TRD)
Bipolar II disorder depressive phase (frequently misdiagnosed as TRD — critical to exclude)
Persistent depressive disorder (dysthymia) misclassified as TRD
Substance-induced mood disorder (active alcohol or substance use confounding treatment)
Undiagnosed medical condition (hypothyroidism, Addison's disease, sleep apnoea — must be excluded)
Treatment non-adherence masquerading as treatment failure
Personality disorder as primary diagnosis with depressive features
PTSD with major depressive episode (requires trauma-focused treatment first)

patient_friendly_explanation.what_is_it: Treatment-Resistant Depression (TRD) is a term used when depression does not get better even after trying two or more antidepressant medications at the right dose for long enough. It does not mean that you cannot be helped — it means that standard treatments have not worked well enough for you and that more specialised options are needed.
patient_friendly_explanation.how_it_affects_you: TRD can feel particularly isolating because the usual treatments have not provided the relief you hoped for. The persistent low mood, loss of enjoyment, exhaustion, and difficulty concentrating can make daily life extremely challenging. Many people with TRD also experience a sense of hopelessness about getting better — but specialised brain stimulation treatments have helped many people in exactly your situation.
patient_friendly_explanation.treatment_options_overview: For people with TRD, there are highly specialised brain stimulation treatments that go beyond what standard medication can offer. The most established options include: accelerated TMS (a compressed, intensive version of magnetic brain stimulation that can achieve rapid results), Deep TMS (which stimulates deeper brain structures), and VNS (a small implanted device that continuously stimulates the vagus nerve to support mood recovery over months). These treatments are supported by strong clinical evidence specifically in treatment-resistant populations.
patient_friendly_explanation.what_to_expect: For accelerated TMS (the SAINT protocol), you would attend the clinic 10 times per day for 5 days — a concentrated course that some people find very convenient compared to 6 weeks of daily visits. Each session is only 3–4 minutes long. Standard extended TMS courses for TRD involve 30–36 daily sessions. VNS requires a small surgical implant and works slowly over months — it is considered when other treatments have not been sufficient. Your clinical team will work with you to choose the most appropriate option based on your history.

faq.question: Does having TRD mean I will never get better?
faq.answer: No. TRD means that standard antidepressant medications have not worked well enough — not that you cannot improve. Many people with TRD respond very well to specialised brain stimulation treatments. Studies show that 30–40% of TRD patients achieve remission with TMS, and VNS has helped many people who did not respond to other treatments.

faq.question: What is accelerated TMS and how is it different from regular TMS?
faq.answer: Accelerated TMS (also known as the SAINT or Stanford protocol) delivers multiple TMS sessions per day — typically 10 sessions daily for 5 days — rather than one session per day over 4–6 weeks. Research suggests this intensive approach can produce very rapid improvements in mood, sometimes within just a few days. It is particularly promising for TRD.

faq.question: What is VNS and who is it for?
faq.answer: VNS (Vagus Nerve Stimulation) involves a small device — about the size of a large coin — implanted under the skin of the chest, with a lead that connects to the left vagus nerve in the neck. It sends gentle electrical pulses to the brain via the vagus nerve to help regulate mood circuits. VNS is specifically approved by the FDA for people with TRD who have not responded to at least four antidepressant treatments. It works slowly over months and requires a surgical procedure.

faq.question: Will I need to stop my current medications for brain stimulation treatment?
faq.answer: Usually no. Most patients continue their current medications during brain stimulation treatment. Your clinical team will review your medication list carefully for any safety considerations. Do not change your medication without medical advice.

faq.question: Is deep brain stimulation (DBS) an option for me?
faq.answer: DBS for depression is currently experimental and is only performed within research trials at specialist centres. It involves implanting electrodes deep in the brain under general anaesthesia. It is not yet a standard clinical treatment outside of research settings. Your clinician can advise if you might be eligible for a research trial.

required_fields.field: Confirmed MDD diagnosis with TRD specifier (≥2 adequate antidepressant trials documented)
required_fields.reason: TRD classification requires documentation of treatment failure history. Protocol access depends on confirmed TRD status.

required_fields.field: Full antidepressant trial history (drug name, class, dose, duration, adherence, response, reason for discontinuation — for each trial)
required_fields.reason: TRD staging (Maudsley Staging Model) requires complete treatment history. Must confirm trials were adequate (therapeutic dose, ≥4 weeks). Pseudo-TRD from inadequate trials must be excluded.
Maudsley Staging (I-IV) quantifies treatment resistance severity and guides protocol intensity and VNS eligibility (Stage IV+ threshold).

required_fields.field: Bipolar disorder screening (MDQ + structured clinical interview)
required_fields.reason: Bipolar II depression is frequently misclassified as TRD. Bipolar disorder is an absolute contraindication for excitatory left DLPFC TMS without specialist oversight.

required_fields.field: Seizure history (any lifetime seizure or epilepsy diagnosis)
Metal implants in head, neck, or upper body
required_fields.reason: Absolute contraindication for TMS. For VNS, implant details and interaction review required.

required_fields.field: Prior TMS history (protocol, number of sessions, response, adverse events)
required_fields.reason: Prior TMS non-response informs protocol selection for retreatment. Accelerated or bilateral protocols may be more appropriate for TMS non-responders.

required_fields.field: Prior ECT history (dates, number of treatments, response, complications)
required_fields.reason: Prior ECT affects seizure threshold estimation and rMT determination. ECT response informs prognosis for TMS.

required_fields.field: Current psychiatric medications (full list — dose, class, prescriber)
required_fields.reason: Medication review for seizure threshold lowering agents required before TMS. Medications affecting VNS efficacy (MAOIs, high-dose benzodiazepines) must be documented.

required_fields.field: Suicidal ideation assessment (C-SSRS — current and historical)
required_fields.reason: TRD is associated with elevated chronic suicide risk. Active suicidal ideation with plan blocks outpatient treatment initiation.

required_fields.field: Cardiac and surgical history (for VNS candidates)
required_fields.reason: VNS implantation requires surgical fitness assessment. Cardiac arrhythmia history may affect VNS parameter selection.

clinical_interview_checklist: Full chronological treatment history: list every antidepressant tried, dose, duration, adherence, and reason for stopping
Classification of each prior trial as adequate (therapeutic dose, ≥4 weeks) or inadequate — to exclude pseudo-TRD
Prior augmentation strategies (lithium, atypical antipsychotics, thyroid hormone, lamotrigine)
Prior psychotherapy and response (CBT, IPT, psychodynamic — duration and response)
Prior neuromodulation history (TMS — protocol, sessions, response; ECT — response, cognition effects)
Current depressive episode duration and functional status
Suicidality assessment — current ideation, plan, intent, prior attempts, means access
Substance use history and current use (active alcohol use is an important treatment confounder)
Psychiatric hospitalisation history (number, duration, voluntary vs involuntary)
Bipolar spectrum features (lifetime hypomanic symptoms, family history of bipolar)
Cognitive complaints (memory, concentration, processing speed — TRD may include cognitive impairment)
Social support assessment (important for VNS candidates who require post-surgical follow-up)
Patient goals and priorities for treatment (speed vs durability, invasiveness preferences)
rule_out_differentials: Bipolar II disorder (hypomanic episodes may be subtle or unrecognised — systematic screen essential)
Pseudo-TRD from inadequate antidepressant trials (sub-therapeutic dose, insufficient duration, or poor adherence)
Undiagnosed hypothyroidism (TSH must be current — even subclinical hypothyroidism impairs antidepressant response)
Active substance use disorder (alcohol in particular — lowers TMS response rates significantly)
PTSD as primary diagnosis with secondary depression (TRD-like presentation; trauma-focused treatment should precede or run concurrently)
Personality disorder as the primary disorder driving depressive features
Sleep apnoea causing treatment-refractory mood symptoms (polysomnography if suspected)
Occult medical condition (inflammatory, autoimmune, adrenal insufficiency)
comorbidity_screen: Anxiety disorders — GAD-7 (comorbid anxiety is a treatment modifier for TRD)
PTSD — PCL-5 (needs trauma-focused treatment concurrently or sequentially)
Alcohol use disorder — AUDIT-C (active alcohol use is a key treatment response predictor)
Sleep disorder — ISI and STOP-BANG (sleep apnoea is underdiagnosed in TRD)
Chronic pain — brief pain inventory (pain-depression interaction affects outcomes)
Cognitive impairment — MoCA (TRD may include persistent cognitive deficits)
prior_treatment_required: Minimum of two documented adequate antidepressant trials (different pharmacological classes, therapeutic dose, ≥4 weeks each) required for TRD classification
For VNS candidacy: minimum of four documented adequate antidepressant trials required (FDA PMA criterion)
Documentation of augmentation strategies attempted (lithium, atypical antipsychotics, thyroid hormone)
Prior TMS and/or ECT history with response details

additional_requirements: Surgical fitness assessment by implanting surgeon
Cardiac evaluation (baseline ECG, cardiology clearance if arrhythmia history)
Informed consent for surgical procedure including anaesthetic risk
Patient understanding of delayed onset of VNS antidepressant effect (weeks to months)
Formal ethics committee (IRB/REC) approval and enrolment in an approved trial
Minimum prior treatment failure threshold as defined by specific trial protocol
Cognitive screening (MoCA or MMSE) to document baseline — TRD in older adults often has a cognitive component
Cardiovascular risk assessment for VNS candidacy
Polypharmacy review for seizure threshold effects
Falls risk assessment before TMS chair positioning

absolute.condition: Ferromagnetic or electronically active implants within the TMS coil field (cochlear implants, aneurysm clips, DBS leads, intracranial electrodes)
absolute.rationale: TMS magnetic field can displace implants or disrupt implanted electronic devices, causing serious injury. Pre-existing DBS systems require device-specific consultation before any TMS.

absolute.condition: Active seizure disorder or seizure within past 12 months (for TMS-based protocols)
absolute.rationale: TMS lowers seizure threshold; active epilepsy substantially elevates seizure induction risk.

absolute.condition: Confirmed bipolar I disorder without specialist co-management and bipolar-specific TMS protocol
absolute.rationale: Excitatory left DLPFC stimulation risks precipitating manic switch in bipolar disorder.

absolute.condition: Implanted cardiac pacemaker or defibrillator (for TMS protocols)
absolute.rationale: TMS electromagnetic field can interfere with device function and cause device failure or inappropriate discharge.

absolute.condition: Active suicidal ideation with specific plan and intent, without adequate safety management in place
absolute.rationale: TRD patients have elevated suicide risk; outpatient treatment requires baseline safety infrastructure. Active crisis requires inpatient or crisis-level intervention first.

absolute.condition: Pregnancy (for implanted VNS) — relative for TMS
absolute.rationale: VNS surgical implantation is contraindicated in pregnancy. TMS in pregnancy requires individual risk-benefit assessment.

relative.condition: History of controlled seizure disorder (>12 months seizure-free on stable medication)
relative.rationale: Residual seizure risk even if controlled.
relative.mitigation: Neurologist clearance required. Conservative TMS parameters. Emergency protocol in place. Monitor for seizure threshold changes with medication adjustments.

relative.rationale: Retreatment with identical parameters has lower expected response rate.
relative.mitigation: Consider protocol modification: accelerated TMS (SAINT), bilateral protocol, Deep TMS H1, or MRI-guided targeting. Document rationale for retreatment.

relative.rationale: Alcohol lowers seizure threshold and substantially impairs TMS antidepressant response rates.
relative.mitigation: Sobriety requirement and alcohol use monitoring throughout. Consider alcohol use disorder treatment concurrently. Document risk-benefit discussion.

relative.condition: High-dose medications lowering seizure threshold (clozapine, bupropion SR >300mg, tricyclics at high doses)
relative.rationale: Pharmacological seizure threshold lowering increases TMS-related seizure risk.
relative.mitigation: Psychiatrist review of medication risk. Consider dose reduction or protocol modification. Conservative stimulation parameters.

relative.condition: Previous non-response to rTMS (one prior course)
relative.rationale: Prior TMS non-response does not preclude benefit from a different protocol or modality.
relative.mitigation: Document prior protocol parameters. Consider accelerated iTBS (SAINT), bilateral protocol, deep TMS (BrainsWay H1), or transition to VNS evaluation.

relative.rationale: VNS stimulates the vagus nerve which has cardiac effects; pre-existing arrhythmia requires careful parameter management.
relative.mitigation: Cardiology clearance required before VNS implantation. Cardiac monitoring during VNS titration. Start at lowest effective parameters.

special_populations.flag: Prior ECT may lower seizure threshold. Document ECT history before TMS motor threshold determination. Wait ≥4 weeks after last ECT before commencing TMS.

special_populations.population: Patients with implanted VNS device being considered for TMS
special_populations.flag: Interaction between TMS field and VNS lead requires device manufacturer consultation. TMS coil must not be placed near the VNS device or lead. Restrict stimulation to brain only, at safe distance from lead.

vns-implanted: Active cardiac arrhythmia requiring investigation before VNS titration
Previous left cervical surgery that may complicate implantation or lead placement
Sleep apnoea that may worsen with VNS stimulation affecting upper airway musculature — screen and manage before implant
Swallowing dysfunction — VNS can cause hoarseness or dysphagia at higher parameters
rtms: Metal implants in TMS coil field

screening.name: Antidepressant Treatment History Form (ATHF)
screening.rationale: Structured tool to document and quantify antidepressant trial adequacy for TRD classification. Essential for Maudsley Staging.

screening.name: MDQ
screening.rationale: Bipolar disorder screening is mandatory before TRD diagnosis is confirmed and before excitatory TMS is initiated.

diagnostic.name: Maudsley Staging Model
diagnostic.full_name: Maudsley Staging Model for Treatment-Resistant Depression
diagnostic.rationale: Quantifies TRD severity across dimensions of treatment failures, symptom duration, and severity. Scores Stage I–IV. Stage IV (score ≥7) informs VNS candidacy.

diagnostic.name: HAM-D17
diagnostic.rationale: Primary clinician-rated severity outcome for TRD trials. Remission = HAM-D ≤7.

diagnostic.name: MADRS
diagnostic.rationale: Sensitive to change; primary outcome in many TRD trials. Remission = MADRS ≤10.

baseline.name: PHQ-9
baseline.rationale: Self-report depression severity for TRD baseline and monitoring. Response = ≥50% reduction; remission = ≤4.

baseline.name: QIDS-SR
baseline.full_name: Quick Inventory of Depressive Symptomatology — Self-Report
baseline.rationale: 16-item self-report capturing all MDD symptom domains; sensitive to change during TMS course.

baseline.name: C-SSRS
baseline.rationale: TRD is associated with elevated chronic suicide risk. C-SSRS baseline and ongoing monitoring is mandatory.

baseline.name: GAD-7
baseline.rationale: Comorbid anxiety is nearly universal in TRD and modifies protocol selection and expected response.

baseline.name: MoCA
baseline.rationale: Cognitive impairment is common in TRD and must be documented at baseline — particularly important for older adults and ECT candidates.

monitoring.name: PHQ-9
monitoring.rationale: Repeated every 5 sessions to track response trajectory during TMS or every 4 weeks during VNS titration.

monitoring.name: C-SSRS
monitoring.rationale: Weekly suicidality monitoring throughout treatment. TRD patients carry higher baseline suicide risk.

outcome.name: PHQ-9
outcome.rationale: End-of-course primary outcome comparison to baseline.

outcome.name: SDS
outcome.rationale: Functional outcomes are a key secondary endpoint in TRD given the chronic functional impairment associated with the condition.

neurophysiological.name: qEEG Frontal Asymmetry Index
neurophysiological.rationale: Guides DLPFC target selection and monitors neurophysiological response to TMS. Particularly valuable for TRD where standard protocol may need biomarker-guided optimisation.

functional.name: SDS
functional.rationale: Functional impairment measure — chronic TRD often causes severe functional disability that is a key treatment target.

protocol_bundle.name: Accelerated iTBS — SAINT Protocol for TRD
protocol_bundle.evidence_summary: The Stanford Accelerated Intelligent Neuromodulation Therapy (SAINT) protocol delivers 10 iTBS sessions per day for 5 consecutive days (50 sessions, 90,000 pulses total) with MRI-guided targeting of the DLPFC region functionally anti-correlated with the sgACC. The pivotal RCT (Cole et al., 2022, NEJM Evidence) demonstrated 79% remission in TRD versus 13% sham. Open-label data show 90%+ remission rates. FDA breakthrough designation received. Represents a major advance for TRD with rapid onset (within 5 days).
references: Cole EJ et al. (2022). Stanford Neuromodulation Therapy (SNT): A Double-Blind Randomized Controlled Trial. NEJM Evidence 1(1).
Cole EJ et al. (2020). Stanford Accelerated Intelligent Neuromodulation Therapy for Treatment-Resistant Depression. Am J Psychiatry 177(8):716-726.
Sackeim HA (2001). The definition and meaning of treatment-resistant depression. J Clin Psychiatry 62(Suppl 16):10-17.

brain_target.region: Left Dorsolateral Prefrontal Cortex (MRI-guided, sgACC anti-correlated site)

parameters.frequency_hz: Theta burst: 50 Hz bursts of 3 pulses at 5 Hz (intermittent: 2s ON / 8s OFF)
parameters.electrode_placement: Left DLPFC — MRI-guided targeting of DLPFC voxel maximally anti-correlated with sgACC (default: F3 without MRI)

additional_params.mri_guidance: Preferred — MRI-guided targeting improves remission rates; neuronavigation strongly recommended

session_structure.maintenance_sessions: Maintenance course consideration at 1–3 months post-remission based on patient trajectory

steps.title: Pre-day assessment
steps.detail: Each treatment day begins with brief mood rating (PHQ-9 or QIDS-SR) and C-SSRS check. Vital signs. Confirm rMT from previous day or re-determine if >24h gap or patient reports significant change. Review overnight adverse events.

steps.title: Session setup
steps.detail: Position patient, confirm neuronavigation alignment (if MRI-guided) or F3 placement (without MRI). Set parameters: 90% rMT, iTBS protocol (1800 pulses). Session lasts 3.5 minutes active stimulation.

steps.title: Active iTBS stimulation
steps.detail: Deliver 1800 pulses iTBS. Observe for seizure, discomfort, or vasovagal response. Document session.

steps.title: Inter-session break
steps.detail: Mandatory ≥50-minute break between sessions per SAINT protocol. Patient may rest in the clinic or waiting area. Monitor for any post-stimulation adverse effects.

steps.title: End-of-day review
steps.detail: After all 10 sessions: mood rating, adverse event check, QIDS-SR or PHQ-9. Document daily progress in treatment record. Escalate to clinician if any safety concern.

monitoring_required: PHQ-9 or QIDS-SR at start of each treatment day
Adverse event log after each individual session
rMT confirmation each morning before first session
Clinician review at start of each treatment day
protocol_bundle.adverse_event_monitoring: Same adverse event profile as standard iTBS. Accelerated schedule increases fatigue risk. Headache may accumulate across sessions within a day — manage with paracetamol; reduce intensity by 5% if persistent. Seizure risk is very low but emergency protocol must be in place for the full 5-day course. Monitor for manic switch daily.
escalation_rules: Any seizure: stop all sessions immediately; emergency protocol; neurologist referral; do not resume without clearance
New suicidal ideation with plan: suspend remaining sessions; crisis protocol; do not allow patient to leave alone
Severe headache not resolving between sessions: pause day; reduce intensity; clinician review
Manic symptoms emerging: suspend all excitatory protocols; psychiatry review

governance.notes: SAINT protocol requires dual-clinician review given the intensive schedule. MRI neuronavigation strongly recommended for target optimisation. FDA breakthrough device designation received.

protocol_bundle.name: Extended rTMS Bilateral Protocol for TRD
protocol_bundle.evidence_summary: Extended course bilateral TMS (right 1Hz inhibitory + left 10Hz excitatory) for patients who did not achieve adequate response from a standard 20–30 session unilateral course. Multiple studies support bilateral protocols for non-responders to unilateral TMS. Course extended to 30–36 sessions. EV-A based on body of TMS evidence for MDD/TRD.
references: Berlim MT et al. (2013). Blinding integrity in randomized sham-controlled trials of repetitive transcranial magnetic stimulation. Int J Neuropsychopharmacol 16(5):1173-80.

Carpenter LL et al. (2012). Transcranial magnetic stimulation (TMS) for major depression: a multisite, naturalistic, observational study of acute treatment outcomes in clinical practice. Depress Anxiety 29(7):587-96.
Pallanti S & Bernardi S (2009). Neurobiology of repeated transcranial magnetic stimulation in the treatment of anxiety. Clin Neurophysiol 120(10):1718-23.

brain_target.region: Left DLPFC (excitatory) and Right DLPFC (inhibitory) — bilateral

parameters.frequency_hz: Left DLPFC: 10 Hz excitatory; Right DLPFC: 1 Hz inhibitory
parameters.pulse_count: 3000 pulses left DLPFC + 1200 pulses right DLPFC = 4200 pulses total per session
parameters.electrode_placement: Left: F3 (10 Hz, 3000 pulses); Right: F4 (1 Hz, 1200 pulses)

session_structure.maintenance_sessions: Maintenance TMS 1x/week for 4 weeks after acute course if response achieved

steps.title: Pre-session check
steps.detail: Patient ID, adverse event review, PHQ-9 every 5 sessions, C-SSRS weekly. Confirm medications unchanged.

steps.title: Right DLPFC 1 Hz inhibitory stimulation
steps.detail: Position coil at F4 (right DLPFC). Deliver 1 Hz at 110% rMT for 20 minutes (1200 pulses). Continuous delivery. Observe patient.

steps.title: Coil repositioning and brief rest
steps.detail: Reposition coil to F3 (left DLPFC). Brief patient check and rest between sides.

steps.title: Left DLPFC 10 Hz excitatory stimulation
steps.detail: Deliver 10 Hz at 120% rMT, 75 trains x 4s ON/26s OFF, 3000 pulses total. Observe for seizure and adverse events.

steps.title: Post-session debrief
steps.detail: Document adverse events, scalp comfort, headache rating. Patient discharged.

monitoring_required: rMT re-check at session 1 and after gaps >2 weeks
protocol_bundle.adverse_event_monitoring: Same as standard rTMS for each component. Longer session duration increases patient fatigue risk. Scalp discomfort may be greater with bilateral protocol — manage with coil angle adjustment and intensity reduction as needed.
escalation_rules: No response at session 18: clinician review — consider accelerated protocol or VNS referral
New suicidal ideation with plan: suspend outpatient treatment; crisis protocol

governance.notes: FDA-cleared for MDD/TRD (rTMS on-label). Bilateral protocol is a clinical decision; parameters within cleared range.

protocol_bundle.name: Implanted Vagus Nerve Stimulation (VNS) for TRD
protocol_bundle.evidence_summary: LivaNova AspireSR VNS received FDA PMA approval specifically for TRD in 2005, with a landmark D-21 study (Rush et al., 2005) and long-term follow-up data demonstrating sustained antidepressant response over 1–2 years. PMA approval requires ≥4 adequate antidepressant treatment failures. The D-21 pivotal trial showed 27% response rate (vs 13% sham) at 1 year; long-term follow-up shows 53% response at 5 years. VNS works via ascending vagal afferent pathways modulating locus coeruleus, amygdala, and frontal cortical circuits.
references: Rush AJ et al. (2005). Vagus nerve stimulation (VNS) for treatment-resistant depressions: a multicenter study. Biol Psychiatry 58(5):347-54.
George MS et al. (2005). A one-year comparison of vagus nerve stimulation with treatment as usual for treatment-resistant depression. Biol Psychiatry 58(5):364-73.
FDA PMA Approval P970003 — LivaNova VNS Therapy System for TRD (2005).
Aaronson ST et al. (2017). A 5-Year Observational Study of Patients With Treatment-Resistant Depression Treated With Vagus Nerve Stimulation. J Clin Psychiatry 78(5):e498-e505.

brain_target.region: Left Vagus Nerve — ascending to locus coeruleus, amygdala, and prefrontal cortex

parameters.intensity: Starting 0.25 mA, titrated to 1.0–1.5 mA (range 0.5–3.5 mA)
parameters.electrode_placement: Left vagus nerve (cervical, lead implanted surgically around left vagus nerve in neck)

additional_params.duty_cycle: 30 seconds ON / 5 minutes OFF (standard cycling mode)
additional_params.titration_schedule: Increase by 0.25 mA every 2 weeks as tolerated at programming visits
additional_params.onset_of_action: Antidepressant response: weeks to months; optimal response typically 6–12 months
additional_params.implant_site: Generator implanted subcutaneously in left chest wall; lead tunnelled to left vagus nerve in neck

session_structure.maintenance_sessions: VNS is continuous — device runs 24/7. Programming visits monthly for first 6 months, then quarterly. Device battery life 5–10 years (generator replacement required).

steps.title: Pre-implant workup and surgical planning
steps.detail: Psychiatric confirmation of TRD criteria (≥4 failures). Surgical fitness assessment. Cardiac evaluation. Informed consent for surgical procedure. Pre-operative bloods, ECG, and anaesthetic review.

steps.title: Surgical implantation (performed by neurosurgeon)
steps.detail: Implant generator subcutaneously in left chest under general or local anaesthesia. Tunnel lead to left cervical vagus nerve. Connect lead to nerve via helical electrodes. Test impedance intra-operatively. Wound closure and dressing.

steps.title: Post-surgical programming (2-week post-op visit)
steps.detail: First device activation at 2 weeks post-surgery (wound healing). Initial parameters: 0.25 mA, 30 Hz, 500 µs, 30s ON/5min OFF. Programme using LivaNova programmer wand. Assess for voice changes, hoarseness (common at higher parameters), or cough.

steps.title: Monthly programming visits (months 1–6)
steps.detail: Assess tolerability and response. Increase current by 0.25 mA if tolerated and no adequate response. Target 1.0–1.5 mA for most patients. Monitor for adverse effects: hoarseness, cough, dyspnoea, bradycardia. Administer PHQ-9 and C-SSRS.

steps.title: Quarterly monitoring (months 7 onwards)
steps.detail: Ongoing quarterly visits: assess response, battery life, tolerability, and need for parameter adjustment. Continue psychiatric medications as directed. Document response trajectory.

monitoring_required: PHQ-9 monthly for first 6 months, quarterly thereafter
Device impedance check at each programming visit
Voice quality assessment at each programming visit
Cardiac monitoring at titration visits if arrhythmia history
protocol_bundle.adverse_event_monitoring: Most common: voice alteration or hoarseness (60–70% of patients — dose-dependent, often improves at lower parameters), cough, neck discomfort. Uncommon: dyspnoea, dysphagia, bradycardia. Rare: infection at implant site. Device must be deactivated with magnet for any required surgical procedures (including MRI — conditionally MRI-compatible device; check device-specific MRI guidelines). Suicidality must be monitored during the lag period before response onset (weeks to months).
escalation_rules: Worsening depression during first 3 months (expected lag period): maintain current parameters; intensify psychiatric support; do not abandon device
Bradycardia or arrhythmia at programming: reduce parameters immediately; cardiology review
New suicidal ideation with plan during lag period: crisis protocol; enhanced monitoring; consider inpatient assessment
Infection at implant site: urgent surgical review; antibiotic therapy; consider explant if severe

governance.notes: FDA PMA approved specifically for TRD (≥4 treatment failures). Requires psychiatric and surgical co-management. Device programming by trained implanting team.

protocol_bundle.notes: VNS for TRD is characterised by a delayed onset of antidepressant effect — patients and families must be counselled on the weeks-to-months timeline for response. Maximum response may take 12–24 months. Do not discontinue based on absence of early response if within the expected lag period.

protocol_bundle.name: Deep TMS H1 Coil (BrainsWay) — TRD Extended Protocol
protocol_bundle.evidence_summary: BrainsWay Deep TMS with H1 coil is FDA-cleared for MDD and is used in TRD populations. Deep TMS uses a helmet-mounted H1 coil that stimulates a broader and deeper DLPFC volume than standard figure-8 coils. The pivotal MDD trial (Levkovitz et al., 2015) and subsequent naturalistic studies demonstrate response rates of 38–53% in medication-resistant populations. Deep TMS penetrates 3–4 cm beneath the scalp compared to 1.5–2 cm for figure-8 coils.
references: Levkovitz Y et al. (2015). Efficacy and safety of deep transcranial magnetic stimulation for major depression: a prospective multicenter randomized controlled trial. World Psychiatry 14(1):64-73.
Tendler A et al. (2016). Deep Transcranial Magnetic Stimulation (Deep TMS) for OCD and Depression. Psychiatry Res 243:139-46.
FDA 510(k) clearance — BrainsWay Deep TMS System H1 coil for MDD.

brain_target.region: Left Dorsolateral Prefrontal Cortex (deep stimulation via H1 coil)

parameters.pulse_count: 2232 pulses per session (62 trains x 2s at 18 Hz = 36 pulses/train)
parameters.electrode_placement: BrainsWay helmet — H1 coil centred over left DLPFC

session_structure.maintenance_sessions: As-needed retreatment at relapse or scheduled quarterly maintenance

steps.title: Pre-session check
steps.detail: Patient ID, adverse event review, PHQ-9 every 5 sessions, C-SSRS weekly.

steps.title: Helmet fitting and motor threshold determination
steps.detail: Fit BrainsWay helmet to patient head. Determine motor threshold using H1 coil over motor cortex. Set stimulation intensity to 120% MT.

steps.title: Active Deep TMS stimulation
steps.detail: 18 Hz, 120% MT, 62 trains (2s ON/20s OFF) = 2232 pulses. Patient seated. Monitor throughout.

steps.title: Post-session debrief
steps.detail: Adverse event check, scalp comfort rating, patient discharge.

monitoring_required: Motor threshold re-check after gaps >2 weeks
protocol_bundle.adverse_event_monitoring: Similar to standard rTMS. H1 coil stimulates deeper and broader cortical volume — may cause slightly more scalp discomfort. Headache is most common adverse event. Seizure risk very low.
escalation_rules: Non-response at session 15: clinician review; consider escalation to VNS referral or SAINT protocol
New suicidal ideation with plan: suspend outpatient treatment; crisis protocol

governance.notes: FDA-cleared for MDD — applicable to TRD populations within cleared indication. BrainsWay proprietary helmet positioning system used.

device_options.name: NeuroStar Advanced Therapy System
device_options.cleared_indication: Major Depressive Disorder (adult, unipolar, medication-inadequate response)
constraints: Standard figure-8 coil — shallower penetration than BrainsWay H1
Motor threshold determination required at first session
device_options.notes: FDA-cleared for MDD. Applicable to TRD within cleared indication.

device_options.name: MagVenture MagPro X100 with Cool-B65 A/P Coil
constraints: MRI neuronavigation integration available for SAINT protocol targeting
device_options.notes: Preferred for SAINT/accelerated iTBS protocols due to cooling capability and compatibility with neuronavigation.

device_options.name: BrainsWay Deep TMS H1 System
device_options.cleared_indication: Major Depressive Disorder — Deep TMS with H1 coil
constraints: Proprietary helmet positioning system — no neuronavigation required
H1 coil penetrates 3–4 cm vs 1.5–2 cm for standard figure-8
Motor threshold determined using H1 coil — different from standard rTMS MT determination
device_options.notes: FDA-cleared for MDD. Used in TRD extended protocols. Helmet system ensures consistent positioning without neuronavigation.

device_options.name: LivaNova AspireSR VNS Therapy Generator
device_options.cleared_indication: Treatment-Resistant Depression (≥4 adequate antidepressant failures) — FDA PMA approved
constraints: Surgical implantation required — performed by trained neurosurgeon
MRI conditional — check device-specific MRI compatibility guidelines before any MRI scan
Battery life 5–10 years — generator replacement surgery required
Device must be deactivated during diathermy, therapeutic ultrasound, or other medical procedures
Programming requires proprietary LivaNova programmer wand
device_options.notes: FDA PMA approved specifically for TRD (≥4 treatment failures). Only device with a specific TRD PMA approval. AspireSR model includes AutoStim cardiac-based sensing capability.

brain_targets.rationale: Primary excitatory TMS target for TRD. In MRI-guided SAINT protocol, the DLPFC subregion maximally anti-correlated with the sgACC is selected for optimal circuit engagement and superior remission rates.
brain_targets.qeeg_target: Reduce left frontal alpha excess at F3; normalise frontal alpha asymmetry score
brain_targets.evidence_note: MRI-guided targeting of DLPFC subregion anti-correlated with sgACC improves TRD remission rates significantly (Fox et al., 2012).

brain_targets.rationale: Inhibitory low-frequency (1 Hz) rTMS to the right DLPFC reduces relative right-sided hyperactivation and is used as the inhibitory component of bilateral TRD protocols.
brain_targets.qeeg_target: Reduce relative right frontal hyperactivity marker
brain_targets.evidence_note: Used in bilateral protocols as inhibitory component alongside left excitatory stimulation.

brain_targets.rationale: The sgACC (BA25) is chronically hyperactive in TRD and drives negative affect, rumination, and autonomic dysregulation. It is the primary surgical DBS target for TRD. In TMS, it is targeted indirectly via DLPFC connectivity or directly via MRI-guided targeting of DLPFC anti-correlated with sgACC.
brain_targets.qeeg_target: Reduce midline theta (Fz) as indirect marker of sgACC hyperactivity
brain_targets.evidence_note: Fox et al. (2012) demonstrated that DLPFC subregions most anti-correlated with sgACC produce superior TMS antidepressant effects.

brain_targets.rationale: VNS targets the left vagus nerve to deliver ascending neuromodulatory signals to the locus coeruleus (noradrenergic system), nucleus tractus solitarius, thalamus, amygdala, and prefrontal cortex. Left-sided stimulation preferred to avoid cardiac bradycardia risk associated with right VNS.
brain_targets.network: Ascending neuromodulatory systems — locus coeruleus, limbic network
brain_targets.evidence_note: FDA PMA approved VNS target for TRD (≥4 treatment failures). LivaNova AspireSR device. Left vagus nerve stimulation preferred over right due to reduced cardiac side effects.

sections.title: Defining and Staging TRD
content: TRD is defined as MDD that has failed ≥2 adequate antidepressant trials of different pharmacological classes, each at therapeutic dose for ≥4 weeks with documented adequate adherence.

The Maudsley Staging Model (Stage I–IV) quantifies TRD severity incorporating number of treatment failures, treatment duration, and symptom severity. Stage IV (score ≥7) corresponds to ≥4 adequate treatment failures and meets the FDA PMA criterion for VNS eligibility.
Pseudo-TRD (apparent treatment resistance due to inadequate dose, duration, or adherence) must be systematically excluded before TRD classification is confirmed.

Bipolar disorder must be formally excluded at TRD diagnosis — Bipolar II depressive phase is frequently misclassified as TRD and excitatory left DLPFC TMS carries manic switch risk in this population.

sections.title: Protocol Selection Algorithm
content: Maudsley Stage II (2 failed trials): Standard TMS/iTBS extended course (PRO-TMS-TRD-02 or PRO-DTMS-TRD-01). Consider SAINT accelerated protocol if rapid response is clinically prioritised.
Maudsley Stage III (3 failed trials, including prior standard TMS): Accelerated SAINT iTBS protocol (PRO-TMS-TRD-01) — 79% remission in pivotal RCT.
Maudsley Stage IV (≥4 failed trials): VNS evaluation (PRO-VNS-TRD-01) — FDA PMA approved. Can be combined with ongoing TMS.
Prior TMS non-response: Switch to Deep TMS H1 coil (deeper penetration), accelerated SAINT protocol (MRI-guided targeting), or bilateral protocol — do not retry same protocol without modification.
DBS for TRD: experimental only; refer to research centres with active trials if all other options exhausted.

sections.title: Enhanced Safety Requirements for TRD
content: TRD patients carry elevated chronic suicide risk compared to treatment-responsive MDD. Enhanced monitoring (weekly C-SSRS) is mandatory throughout.
The lag period for VNS response (weeks to months) represents a particularly vulnerable period — maintain enhanced psychiatric contact and safety monitoring during this phase.
Active alcohol use significantly reduces TMS response rates — address alcohol use as a concurrent treatment priority.
ECT history reduces motor threshold — recalculate rMT carefully in prior ECT patients.
Clinician dual review is required before initiating the SAINT accelerated protocol and VNS implantation.

sections.title: VNS Management and Titration
content: VNS is activated at 2 weeks post-implantation. Initial parameters: 0.25 mA, 30 Hz, 500 µs pulse width, 30s ON / 5 min OFF duty cycle.
Increase current by 0.25 mA every 2 weeks as tolerated. Target therapeutic range: 1.0–1.5 mA for most patients (maximum 3.5 mA).
Voice alteration or hoarseness is present in 60–70% of patients at therapeutic parameters — it is dose-dependent and often manageable with parameter adjustment.
Antidepressant response is expected weeks to months after implantation. Maximum benefit may take 12–24 months. Do not abandon treatment within the first 6 months.
MRI compatibility: LivaNova AspireSR is conditionally MRI-compatible — check device IFU for MRI conditions and required parameter adjustments before any MRI scan.

patient_guide.title: Your Guide to Specialised Treatment for Treatment-Resistant Depression

sections.title: What is Treatment-Resistant Depression?
content: Treatment-Resistant Depression means that your depression has not responded well enough to at least two different antidepressant medications taken at the right dose for long enough. This is not uncommon — about one in three people with depression find that standard medications do not give them enough relief.
Having TRD does not mean you cannot get better. It means you need a more specialised approach, and there are treatments specifically designed for people in your situation.

sections.title: Your Treatment Options
content: Accelerated TMS (SAINT Protocol): An intensive 5-day version of TMS brain stimulation. You attend the clinic for 10 short sessions each day (each session is about 4 minutes), completing 50 sessions in just 5 days. Studies show this rapid approach can produce significant mood improvement within days in many people with TRD.
Standard Extended TMS: The same magnetic brain stimulation as standard TMS but with more sessions (30–36 days) and sometimes a bilateral approach (stimulating both sides of the brain).
Deep TMS: A version of TMS using a helmet-based device that stimulates a larger and deeper area of the brain involved in mood. Sessions last about 20 minutes.

VNS (Vagus Nerve Stimulation): For people who have not responded to four or more treatments. A small device is implanted under the skin of the chest, sending gentle electrical pulses to the brain via the vagus nerve in the neck. It works slowly over months and can produce lasting improvements.

sections.title: What to Expect — Timelines
content: Accelerated TMS (SAINT): 5 days of intensive treatment. Many people notice mood improvements within or shortly after the 5-day course.
Standard TMS: 6–7 weeks of daily clinic visits. Improvements typically begin from week 2–4.

VNS: Requires a short surgical procedure. The device is then programmed over several visits. Mood improvements develop gradually over weeks to months — sometimes up to 12–24 months for maximum benefit. It is important not to give up on VNS in the early weeks.
Keep all appointments and contact your clinical team if you are concerned at any stage — particularly if your mood worsens or you have thoughts of harming yourself.

technician_sop.title: TRD Neuromodulation — Technician Standard Operating Procedure

sections.title: SAINT Accelerated Protocol — Technician Checklist
content: At the start of each treatment day: confirm patient ID (two identifiers), review PHQ-9/QIDS-SR from morning rating, confirm C-SSRS has been completed by clinician, and verify rMT from previous day.
Minimum 50-minute rest between each iTBS session must be adhered to — track time precisely and document.
Between sessions: patient must remain in the clinic or designated waiting area. Monitor for adverse effects, dizziness, or distress.
After all 10 sessions each day: complete daily adverse event log and end-of-day mood rating. Notify clinician if any safety concern has arisen during the day.
Emergency equipment must be immediately accessible throughout the full 5-day course.

sections.title: VNS Programming Visits — Technician Support Role
content: Technician supports the programming clinician by preparing the LivaNova programmer wand and patient record.
Document all programming parameters: current (mA), frequency (Hz), pulse width (µs), ON time, OFF time.
After parameter changes: observe patient for 5 minutes for voice changes, cough, or dyspnoea. Document any adverse effects.
If patient experiences bradycardia or significant respiratory difficulty: notify implanting clinician immediately; have magnet available to temporarily disable device.
Complete programming visit record including impedance readings and patient-reported tolerability.

sections.title: Enhanced Suicide Risk Monitoring for TRD
content: TRD patients have higher baseline suicide risk than standard MDD patients. The C-SSRS must be completed by a clinician every week without exception.
Any patient reporting new suicidal ideation with a plan or intent must not leave the clinic unaccompanied. Notify the supervising clinician immediately.
During the VNS lag period (first 6 months), patients should be contacted by phone between programming visits to check on mood and safety.
Document all suicidality-related conversations and clinical actions in the patient record.

assessment_schedule.timing: Before session 1 (baseline), every 5 sessions for TMS; monthly for VNS; end of course
Before session 1 (baseline), then weekly throughout any TMS course; monthly during VNS follow-up
Baseline, mid-course (session 15 or month 3 VNS), and end of course (month 6, 12 VNS)
At intake only (documents TRD staging at treatment initiation)
Baseline and end of course; 6-month and 12-month follow-up

response_thresholds.non_response_threshold: <20% improvement from baseline at session 15 (TMS) or month 3 (VNS)
response_thresholds.deterioration_threshold: Increase of ≥5 points from any monitoring point

response_thresholds.non_response_threshold: <25% reduction at session 15 (TMS) or month 3 (VNS) — note: VNS response may be delayed; do not classify as non-response before month 6
response_thresholds.deterioration_threshold: Increase of ≥4 points from any monitoring point

adverse_event_triggers.action: Stop stimulation immediately. Emergency protocol. Do not resume without neurologist clearance. Regulatory incident report.

adverse_event_triggers.event_type: New suicidal ideation with plan or intent
adverse_event_triggers.action: Do not allow patient to leave unaccompanied. Immediate clinician notification. Crisis protocol. Suspend outpatient treatment. Safety planning and possible inpatient referral.
Stop VNS stimulation with magnet. Reduce programmed parameters. Cardiology review. Do not increase parameters until cardiology clearance.
Urgent surgical review. Antibiotic therapy. Consider device explantation if deep infection. Do not resume programming until infection resolved.

adverse_event_triggers.event_type: Manic or hypomanic symptoms during treatment
adverse_event_triggers.action: Suspend all excitatory neuromodulation. Psychiatric review within 24 hours. Rule out bipolar disorder. Document fully.

adverse_event_triggers.event_type: Severe headache during accelerated TMS (accumulating across sessions)
adverse_event_triggers.action: Pause remaining sessions for the day. Analgesics. Reduce intensity by 10% for subsequent days. Clinician review.

escalation_rules.trigger: PHQ-9 non-response (<20% improvement) at session 15 of TMS course
escalation_rules.action: Mandatory clinician review. Consider: SAINT accelerated protocol, bilateral protocol switch, Deep TMS H1 coil, or VNS referral evaluation if ≥4 total treatment failures.

escalation_rules.trigger: PHQ-9 deterioration (≥5 points increase from any monitoring point)
escalation_rules.action: Clinician review within 24 hours. Assess for new stressors, medication changes, or emerging bipolar features.

escalation_rules.trigger: New suicidal ideation with plan at any point
escalation_rules.action: Immediate clinician review. Crisis protocol. Suspend outpatient TMS. Inpatient assessment if required. Do not discharge alone.
Emergency services. Neurologist referral. Regulatory incident report. Do not resume without specialist clearance.

escalation_rules.trigger: VNS: No response by month 6 at maximum tolerated parameters
escalation_rules.action: Psychiatric review. Consider combination with TMS. Reassess Maudsley Stage. Discuss DBS research trial eligibility with patient.

escalation_rules.trigger: Missed 3 or more consecutive TMS sessions
escalation_rules.action: Clinical team phone contact within 24 hours. Re-engage or discharge. rMT re-determination at return to treatment.

adherence_rules.action_on_low_adherence: Clinical team contact within 24 hours of third consecutive missed session. Assess barriers to attendance. Consider home-compatible adjuncts. Re-determine rMT on return to treatment. Document discussion in patient record.

sections.title: TRD Classification and Maudsley Staging
sections.clinician_narrative_prompt: Document the Maudsley Staging score and stage (I-IV). List each prior antidepressant trial with adequacy classification (adequate vs inadequate). State the basis for TRD confirmation.

sections.title: Baseline Severity Scores
sections.clinician_narrative_prompt: Summarise baseline severity classification. Comment on chronicity and functional impairment level (SDS).

sections.title: Full Treatment History
sections.clinician_narrative_prompt: Comprehensive treatment history. Flag any pseudo-TRD considerations excluded. Justify why remaining modalities were not used or were tried and failed.

sections.title: Protocol Selection and VNS Eligibility Assessment
sections.clinician_narrative_prompt: Justify the selected protocol. If Maudsley Stage IV: document VNS eligibility assessment and outcome. If VNS deferred, document rationale.

sections.title: Safety Assessment and Risk Management Plan
sections.clinician_narrative_prompt: TRD-specific risk assessment. Document current C-SSRS category, suicide risk stratification, and the agreed risk management plan including emergency contact details.

sections.title: Treatment Progress Summary
sections.clinician_narrative_prompt: Describe overall treatment trajectory. Note any protocol modifications, dose adjustments (VNS), or escalation decisions.

sections.title: Response Tracking
sections.clinician_narrative_prompt: Response classification: responder/partial/non-responder. For VNS, note expected timeline and whether patient is within the expected lag period.

sections.title: Suicidality Update and Safety
sections.clinician_narrative_prompt: TRD-specific: document C-SSRS category, any changes from baseline, and safety interventions if applicable.

sections.title: Adverse Events
sections.clinician_narrative_prompt: For VNS: document voice alteration, hoarseness, cough, or dysphagia. For TMS: document headache, scalp discomfort. Any serious adverse events.

report_templates.name: TRD End-of-Course Report
report_templates.frequency: Once — at end of TMS course or 12-month VNS review

sections.title: Course Summary
sections.clinician_narrative_prompt: Full summary of treatment course including protocol, sessions delivered, and parameter evolution (VNS titration history).

sections.title: Outcome Measures and Clinical Response
sections.clinician_narrative_prompt: Classify outcome: remission, response, partial response, or non-response. Include functional outcome (SDS change). Discuss durability if known.

sections.title: Recommendations and Next Steps
sections.clinician_narrative_prompt: Maintenance plan. If non-response to all TMS protocols: VNS referral assessment or DBS research trial eligibility. Ongoing psychiatric medication management.

report_templates.name: TRD SOAP Note
report_templates.frequency: Each TMS session or monthly VNS programming visit

sections.title: Subjective
sections.clinician_narrative_prompt: Patient's self-reported mood, sleep, energy, and adverse effects since last session. For VNS: note voice changes, cough, or device-related discomfort.

sections.title: Objective
sections.clinician_narrative_prompt: TMS parameters delivered or VNS programming parameters set. Assessment scores if collected today.

sections.title: Assessment
sections.clinician_narrative_prompt: Clinical progress interpretation. Response classification. Any safety concerns noted.

sections.title: Plan
sections.clinician_narrative_prompt: Protocol modifications for next session. Escalation actions if triggered. VNS parameter changes planned at next visit.

home_program_templates.name: TRD Enhanced Home Safety and Wellness Monitoring Program
home_program_templates.device_required: Patient-owned smartphone with clinician-prescribed mood tracking application
patient_instructions: Every morning, rate your mood on a scale of 0 (lowest) to 10 (highest) using your mood tracking app.
Every evening, record your sleep quality from the previous night (1 = very poor, 5 = excellent) and energy level (0–10).
If your mood rating is 3 or below on any morning, contact the clinic by phone during business hours — do not wait for your next appointment.
Keep the clinic's emergency contact number and your local crisis line number saved in your phone.
Take your prescribed medications as directed — never stop or reduce your medication without speaking to your prescribing clinician first.
If you experience new thoughts of harming yourself, call the clinic, a crisis line, or emergency services immediately. Do not act on these thoughts.
Attend all scheduled clinic appointments — consistency is essential for TRD treatment to work.
safety_checklist: I have the clinic emergency contact number saved in my phone.
I have a crisis line number saved in my phone.
I have taken my medications as prescribed today.
I have a trusted person I can contact if I feel unsafe.
I have agreed to contact the clinic or emergency services if my safety is at risk.
reporting_requirements: Daily mood rating (0–10) via mood tracking app
Any adverse effects from treatment since last clinic visit
Any changes to medications or new medical conditions
contraindications_for_home: Active suicidal ideation — call the clinic, crisis line, or emergency services immediately; do not manage alone at home
New onset of manic or hypomanic symptoms — contact the clinic urgently
Severe worsening of depression (mood rating 0–2 for two consecutive mornings) — call the clinic same day
home_program_templates.evidence_note: Structured home monitoring between clinic sessions improves safety outcomes in TRD by enabling earlier identification of deterioration and safety crises during the extended treatment timelines required. Digital mood tracking tools improve engagement and provide objective data for clinical review.

consent_documents.name: Informed Consent for TMS Treatment — Treatment-Resistant Depression
key_disclosures: TMS uses magnetic pulses to stimulate areas of the brain involved in mood regulation.
TMS is FDA-cleared for Major Depressive Disorder in adults; it is used for TRD within this cleared indication.
Response rates in TRD are lower than in first-episode MDD — approximately 30–40% of TRD patients achieve meaningful response with standard TMS.
Accelerated protocols (SAINT/Stanford) show higher remission rates (up to 79% in controlled trials) in TRD.
The most common side effects are scalp discomfort and headache, which are usually mild and temporary.
The most serious risk is seizure (approximately 1 in 1,000 to 10,000 treatment courses); TRD risk factors are carefully screened.
You should continue your psychiatric medications during TMS unless otherwise advised by your prescribing clinician.
You may withdraw consent at any time without affecting your future care.
Given your treatment-resistant status, there is a possibility that TMS will not produce adequate response — alternative options including VNS will be discussed if this occurs.

consent_documents.name: TMS Contraindication Screening Checklist — TRD
consent_documents.when_required: Before first TMS session and at any significant medical change
key_disclosures: History of seizures or epilepsy.
Metal implants in the head or neck (cochlear implants, aneurysm clips, DBS leads).
Implanted cardiac pacemaker or defibrillator.
History of bipolar disorder or manic episodes — systematic screening required for TRD.
Current medications that lower seizure threshold.
Prior ECT (electroconvulsive therapy) — dates, response, complications.
Prior TMS (protocol, sessions, response, adverse events).
Pregnancy or breastfeeding.
Any significant medical change since last session.
consent_documents.review_frequency: Before each new course; reviewed at each session for new symptoms

consent_documents.name: Informed Consent for Implanted VNS Therapy — TRD
consent_documents.when_required: Before VNS surgical implantation — after TRD eligibility confirmed (≥4 adequate treatment failures)
key_disclosures: VNS (Vagus Nerve Stimulation) for TRD requires surgical implantation of a small device under the skin of the chest.
VNS is FDA PMA approved for TRD in adults who have failed at least four adequate antidepressant treatments.
The surgical procedure is performed under general or local anaesthesia and carries standard surgical risks (bleeding, infection, anaesthetic complications).
The device sends gentle electrical pulses to the brain via the left vagus nerve in the neck.
Antidepressant response from VNS is delayed — it typically develops over weeks to months. Maximum benefit may take 12–24 months. Do not judge effectiveness in the first few weeks.
Common side effects include voice alteration or hoarseness (60–70% of patients), cough, and throat discomfort — these are usually manageable with parameter adjustment.
Once implanted, the device runs continuously (30 seconds ON every 5 minutes). The magnet provided can temporarily disable it.
The device battery lasts approximately 5–10 years — a generator replacement procedure will be required at end of battery life.
MRI scans are conditionally compatible — you must inform all future medical teams of your implanted device before any MRI or surgical procedure.
VNS does not guarantee remission. Long-term studies show approximately 53% response at 5 years.
You may request device deactivation or explantation at any time — discuss with your surgical and psychiatric team.
consent_documents.review_frequency: Once before implantation; reviewed annually thereafter

consent_documents.name: Off-Label Treatment Acknowledgement — Accelerated SAINT iTBS Protocol
key_disclosures: The SAINT (Stanford Accelerated Intelligent Neuromodulation Therapy) protocol delivers 10 iTBS sessions per day for 5 days — a more intensive schedule than standard cleared TMS protocols.
While iTBS is FDA-cleared for MDD, the specific accelerated 10-sessions-per-day SAINT schedule constitutes an off-label use of the cleared technology.
The SAINT protocol has received FDA Breakthrough Device Designation and is supported by a double-blind randomised controlled trial (Cole et al., 2022, NEJM Evidence) showing 79% remission in TRD.
Your clinician has recommended this intensive protocol based on your TRD history and clinical need for a potentially rapid response.
You have been informed of and understand the distinction between FDA clearance and the specific SAINT schedule.
You may refuse this protocol and request alternative cleared treatments.

consent_documents.name: Data Sharing Consent — TRD Treatment Outcomes
key_disclosures: Your de-identified treatment data may be used to improve clinical protocols and outcomes research for treatment-resistant depression.
Your data will never be shared in an identifiable form without separate specific consent.
Participation in data sharing is voluntary and does not affect your clinical care.
You may withdraw consent to data sharing at any time by notifying the clinic.
Data is stored securely in compliance with applicable data protection regulations.
TRD outcome data is particularly valuable for advancing care for future patients in similar clinical situations.
